steps.title: Fastest path: mark the figure first
steps.content: Mark the given angle relationships on the figure first: equal angles, vertical angles, supplementary angles, parallel-line angles, and triangle sums of $180^\circ$. Most of these questions become a short angle chase once the givens are marked.

steps.title: Combine the right-side fractions over a common denominator
steps.content:
Starting from $\frac{1}{R} = \frac{1}{p} + \frac{1}{q} + \frac{1}{s} + \frac{1}{13}$, use the common denominator $13pqs$:

$\frac{1}{p} = \frac{13qs}{13pqs}$, $\frac{1}{q} = \frac{13ps}{13pqs}$, $\frac{1}{s} = \frac{13pq}{13pqs}$, $\frac{1}{13} = \frac{pqs}{13pqs}$.

So $\frac{1}{R} = \frac{13qs + 13ps + 13pq + pqs}{13pqs}$.

steps.title: Take reciprocals to solve for R
Eliminate the answer choices
steps.content:
• A uses $p+q+s+13$ in the denominator, which would only be right if $1/R = 4/(p+q+s+13)$ — not equivalent to the given sum of reciprocals.
  • B equals $\frac{13pqs}{13qs+13ps+13pq+pqs}$, exactly what we derived.
  • C is the reciprocal of B, so it equals $\frac{1}{R}$, not $R$.
  • D sums the resistances directly, which is the formula for resistors in series, not parallel.
That confirms B is correct.
